inline_review:
commit bd9e5b9830d33e4223174de5bae22c6a4a89a754
Author: Yang Yingliang <yangyingliang@huawei.com>

mm: vmstat: make refresh interval take effect immediately

The commit changes the stat_interval sysctl handler to apply the new
interval immediately. It does this by directly calling mod_delayed_work
on the shepherd and per-CPU vmstat delayed works when the sysctl is
updated.

> diff --git a/mm/vmstat.c b/mm/vmstat.c
> --- a/mm/vmstat.c
> +++ b/mm/vmstat.c

[ ... ]

> @@ -2152,6 +2155,38 @@ static void vmstat_shepherd(struct work_struct *w)
>  		round_jiffies_relative(sysctl_stat_interval));
>  }
>  
> +#ifdef CONFIG_PROC_FS
> +static int sysctl_stat_interval_handler(const struct ctl_table *table, int write,
> +					void *buffer, size_t *length, loff_t *ppos)
> +{
> +	int cpu;
> +	int ret = proc_dointvec_jiffies(table, write, buffer, length, ppos);
> +
> +	if (ret || !write)
> +		return ret;
> +
> +	cpus_read_lock();
> +	for_each_online_cpu(cpu) {
> +		struct delayed_work *dw = &per_cpu(vmstat_work, cpu);
> +
> +		scoped_guard(rcu) {
> +			if (cpu_is_isolated(cpu))
> +				continue;
> +
> +			if (delayed_work_pending(dw))
> +				mod_delayed_work(system_wq, dw, round_jiffies_relative(sysctl_stat_interval));

Does this queue the per-CPU work items on the wrong CPU and workqueue?

Calling mod_delayed_work() implicitly targets WORK_CPU_UNBOUND and queues
the item on system_wq. Because the vmstat_update() function relies on
this_cpu_* operations, running these works on the single CPU executing the
sysctl handler redundantly updates its own stats while ignoring the intended
target CPUs.

Additionally, mm_percpu_wq is specifically used by vmstat to guarantee
forward progress during out-of-memory conditions via the WQ_MEM_RECLAIM
flag. Switching to system_wq strips this guarantee, which could introduce a
deadlock risk under severe memory pressure.

Should this use mod_delayed_work_on(cpu, mm_percpu_wq, ...) instead?

> +		}
> +
> +		cond_resched();
> +	}
> +	cpus_read_unlock();
> +
> +	mod_delayed_work(system_wq, &shepherd, round_jiffies_relative(sysctl_stat_interval));

Can this execute early in boot if the sysctl is modified via the kernel
command line (e.g., sysctl.vm.stat_interval=...)?

If sysctl_stat_interval_handler() runs before start_shepherd_timer() has
fully initialized the vmstat subsystem, the shepherd work and the per-CPU
vmstat_work structures will still be zero-initialized.

When vmstat_shepherd() executes and queues the per-CPU works during early
boot, queueing a zero-initialized work_struct could corrupt the workqueue
linked lists and trigger a NULL pointer dereference.

> +
> +	return ret;
> +}
> +#endif
output:
{"findings":[{"problem":"Incorrect workqueue and CPU affinity used for per-CPU vmstat works, breaking statistics aggregation and memory reclaim guarantees.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. The sysctl handler uses `mod_delayed_work(system_wq, dw, ...)`, which implicitly targets `WORK_CPU_UNBOUND`. Because `system_wq` is a bound workqueue, the workqueue subsystem will queue all these work items on the single local CPU executing the sysctl handler.
2. When these migrated `vmstat_update` works execute, they rely on `this_cpu_*` operations. As a result, the executing CPU's stats are redundantly updated while the intended target CPUs' stats are completely ignored.
3. Furthermore, `vmstat_update` requeues itself using `this_cpu_ptr(&vmstat_work)`. The target CPUs' work items are never requeued, entirely stalling their periodic updates until rescued by `vmstat_shepherd`.
4. The patch explicitly moves the works from `mm_percpu_wq` to `system_wq`. `mm_percpu_wq` is specifically allocated with the `WQ_MEM_RECLAIM` flag to guarantee forward progress during out-of-memory conditions. Stripping this guarantee introduces a potential deadlock risk under severe memory pressure.
5. To fix this, the patch must use `mod_delayed_work_on(cpu, mm_percpu_wq, dw, round_jiffies_relative(sysctl_stat_interval))`."},{"problem":"Modifying the sysctl early in boot queues uninitialized work structures, leading to a kernel panic.","severity":"High","severity_explanation":"1. If the `stat_interval` sysctl is modified early during boot (e.g., via the kernel command line `sysctl.vm.stat_interval=...`), `sysctl_stat_interval_handler` will execute before the vmstat subsystem is fully initialized.
2. The handler unconditionally calls `mod_delayed_work(system_wq, &shepherd, ...)`, queueing the statically declared `shepherd` delayed work.
3. When `vmstat_shepherd()` executes, it iterates over all online CPUs. If `need_update(cpu)` is true (which is highly likely during early boot memory allocations), it calls `queue_delayed_work_on(cpu, mm_percpu_wq, dw, 0)`.
4. However, the per-CPU `vmstat_work` structures are zero-initialized statically (`DEFINE_PER_CPU(struct delayed_work, vmstat_work)`) and are not dynamically initialized until `start_shepherd_timer()` runs later in the boot process.
5. Queueing a zero-initialized `work_struct` corrupts the workqueue linked lists and triggers a NULL pointer dereference because its function pointer has not yet been set."}],"fixes":"","review_inline":"commit bd9e5b9830d33e4223174de5bae22c6a4a89a754
